speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: You were trying to remember the Latin teacher's name. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oberstudienrat Dr. Ziemmer.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out about him afterward?

speaker: interviewee
text: Afterwards? Because I-- we had a class. I-- 1968, we had an interesting experience. My hatred for Germany was so intense that I swore I will never, ever enter that again, that country again. Shall I tell you this? OK. The-- the girls, the women, the people I went-- the-- and my-- my classmates, we met in 1968 again, and we talked about this, my feelings. And they told me, you're wrong, Ruth. It's absolutely not true. He-- he lost his position. Because he wanted to become principal. They were going to make him principal, and so many people thought he was a Nazi. And then his whole story came out that he was really not a Nazi at all. And then I was very sorry for my thoughts.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your family's religious background?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we were conservative Jews. We had one synagogue in Krefeld, and we attended it on a regular basis, my mother upstairs, my father downstairs. And I spent a lot of time outside with the boys and girls, but I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the population of Krefeld?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then? I think it was 150,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 1,500.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents practice Judaism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We celebrated all the holidays. We did not have a kosher home. On Shabbos, on Shabbat, we-- I went almost every week in my later teen-- teen years. I went almost every Shabbat morning to synagogue. I'm not so sure whether it was because of the praying or because the boys were there. I don't know that for sure. I can't recall. But I was there. And my parents went mostly for holidays, my father not as lovingly as my mother, who used to sit upstairs with her mother. So she-- she went, and my father went because that was the thing to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hanukkah, naturally. That's when we got presents, and we did. We-- we lit the candles and sang. We-- we knew all-- every one of the-- the Maoz Tzur from beginning to end. I don't know how many stanzas there were. And-- and we-- we lit the candles, and that was-- that was always nice, because outside of birthdays, so it was always good. But the-- the-- the Seders when I was little were nice. My aunt, my grandmother's sister, led the Seder. My father didn't know anything about it. He was not brought up in any shape or form in the Jew-- Jewish environment, but nothing-- not religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my aunt used to do it. But then she moved to-- went to America, and I cannot remember any more Seders after that. I think there was a public Seder once from-- from the Gemeinde, from the congregation, that the rabbi did, Dr. Bluhm. Did your mother light candles on Friday night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else was celebrated on Friday night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. On Friday afternoons on a regular basis we took a bath, and then we prepared the couch in the [GERMAN], which was a-- like a living room. And we prepared it because Mutti would sit down with us, my sister and me, and she would tell us a story. And she always started it by saying, do you want a happy one or a sad one? And the answer was always a sad one. And that's how we learned about operas, about classical-- about the classics, because she made that adjusting very wisely to our-- you know, between my sister and I, she kind of had to-- to graduate the age and so that we could understand it. But we-- but they were almost always sad at the end, and that's what we liked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, I do, very well. Not my grand-- my mother's father died when I was three. I do remember him. I remember sitting on his lap, and I remember when he died. And I remember that I was in his bedroom at the very end, and I looked out the window. And there was a little alley, and there was a big cart for one of those vegetable-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] vegetable carts filled with tomatoes. And for years and years, I couldn't touch a tomato. It's strange, this correlation between one event and another. And my-- my grandmother, my mother's mother, I was never very close to. She was a businesswoman. She was a businesswoman when she had her son and-- and her daughter, who was born first, my mother and a son. She had very little to do with their upbringing. And she was about the same when-- with her grandchildren. She was very much to herself, very-- very religious. She davened three times a day, prayed three times a day, very industriously. And She was by-- by herself. She didn't-- she later on moved in with us. My other grandparents I loved very much. I was very close with my father's mother. And reading my diary the last two days, I never even realized how close and how I missed her after-- after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us their names, the grandparents' names.

speaker: interviewee
text: My gran-- my father's parents' name were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Vilhelmina, and everybody called her [? Minchen. ?] And my grandfather's name was-- was Valentin. And my grandmother's maiden name was [? Wolff, ?] with two F's.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your--

speaker: interviewee
text: And she came from [NON-ENGLISH], being in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maternal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was-- Marcus was my grandfather's name. He came from [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], something like this. I don't know where it came. And my grandmother was Amilia, and she was born in-- she comes from-- from [NON-ENGLISH] really, which is just a-- just outside of Krefeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your rich grandmother was living with you after--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's mother. She lost her husband very early. And-- and she had her own place with this aunt who conducted the Seder. They lived together, but then my aunt went to the United States, and my grandmother moved in with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your life change? When did you realize that there are changes in Europe and Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose the first thing that-- first time I [? met ?] notices when I have to leave school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1936, the-- I have wrote it all down, but 1937, yeah, 1937. And that's when I realized that life changed. I couldn't understand. I didn't know what to do next. We could not have maids anymore in the house. We had a very large, three-story house and a living basement where my father had his office. And there was a lot of work, but we were not allowed to have anymore Aryan maids. And so I-- when I left school, I actually worked, did a lot of housework, helped my mother. And there is no comparison to what happened then in-- in '30-- in-- in '38 in the Kristallnacht. That was-- that was the final blow, and that's when I realized I must leave. I must get out. I must see that my family gets out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what happened on Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we heard in the morning of November 8, we-- no, wait. And the synagogues burned on-- on the night. On the morning of the 9th of-- of November, the bell rang about 7 o'clock in the morning. And I-- I ran downstairs so early, and I opened the door, and it was my uncle, my father's brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, what are you doing here so early? What's going on? He said, they burned-- the Nazis burned down our synagogue. Gone And I screamed. I said, that's-- that's-- I can't believe it. He said, it's all totally burned. And-- but relax, relax, everything will be all right. Well, I knew in my mind that was-- everything was not going to be all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then-- then we had to-- moments later, the phone rang. Friends of ours down the street, the Nazis picked up her husband. Another phone-- time the phone rang again, somebody else called, their husband. The women were hysterical. And we were waiting then, they're going to pick up Vati, my father. Our chauffeur came, even though he had already-- my father's business was discontinued. he had to-- he was not able to be-- to work anymore. And our chauffeur had obtained a-- a new job, but that morning he was there. And he said, Mr. Meyer, I'm putting you in the car, and we're going to drive around. No matter what, they're not going to find you. I'll see to it that they won't find you. Now, this man was non-Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We sat all day long in horror waiting for-- we heard the sirens of the police cars, but they didn't stop in front of our house. The afternoon came, and several people, the women, came. My mother went to see some of the women. It was-- it was a horrible situation. And I said, I'm not staying in this house. I'm not staying in this house tonight. I know something is going to happen. And everybody-- and then they-- we listen to the radio, and the announcer kept saying, the synagogues were burned. We don't know who did it. But it's-- this action has been called off. And I said, I don't believe it. We're going to-- I'm-- we-- we must leave the house tonight. Our life is more important than the house. And nobody would listen to me. And we were already at that time talking about leaving the United States. We had a-- we had a number to go to the United States, and the number was very high by the time we heard about it and my father got the number. So-- but my parents prepared. And we had a very large buffet, and my mother-- my mother said, that will never fit into an apartment in-- in-- in Los Angeles-- I mean, in the United States. And she-- it was gone. She had had it cut in half, and it was not in the house that day. All her good dishes were standing on the floor. I'm telling you this for a purpose. And her beautiful, big crystal ball, which they got as a wedding present, a large ball, very beautiful, with a matching plate and a lid, that was on the floor. And I looked at it. I had in my mind something's going to happen tonight. And I took my mother's dishes and this beautiful ball and my diaries and my guitar and things that were important to me, and I went up on the third floor where we had a room, where there was a door on one side, which was a little-- tiny little-- not tiny, but, you know, like the stairs went up to the attic. And underneath these stairs there's always this kind of a room. And I put all these things in there, and then I closed the door. And there was a white wardrobe in that room. This was our ironing room and sewing room when the-- and I moved this cabinet in front of the door. I'm telling you that for a reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we went down, and our chauffeur came again. And he said, I'll take the children with me to my place. And my parents said, you-- you can't do that. What if somebody sees you do this? You'll be in trouble. He said, I won't be. I'm taking them. It's important for me. Because I kept bugging them, bothering them, pleading with them, don't stay in the house. So that's what we did. When it got dark, we kind of took a blanket, wrapped it around ourselves, my sister and I, and went into the car, and he took us to his apartment. He was a hero, that man. I have no doubt about it. His name was Hans Aretz, of all name, Aretz. I always feel there's some kind of a connection there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- and we slept there. I didn't know where my parents went. I could only hope that they would listen to me and leave the house. The next morning, we didn't hear anything. They lived in-- in the center of town, quite a bit away from where we live. And my sister and Mrs. Aretz stood by the window, and she looked out. And when nobody was in the street she said, you can go now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my sister and I and the dog-- we took our dog with us-- walked home, walked towards the house. And when we turned the corner there, we-- we didn't see anything. As we came closer to the house, there was the front door was lying in the middle of the street. And everything was destroyed, everything, except for the things that I hid in that little cubby.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had torn the pipes off the walls. The water was coming out of the pipes without a stop, came down the stairs, went through the ceiling. It was a disaster. I don't have to describe it. Just think a bomb hit the house. That's what it looked like.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we didn't-- I-- I was in charge. I don't even remember what my sister did. She was younger. And I remember saying, yelling, Mutti, Vati, and where are my parents? And again something happened that I seemed to know. And I ran to a friend's house, to some-- there were many people around us. And I ran to their house, and they were there. But how I knew, I don't know, not to this day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father had been to the house before, so my parents already knew it. And we couldn't go back to the house. It was totally demolished. And we had to fix it within two weeks so that-- because the-- the Red Cross was supposed to come, go through the streets. And we were ordered, not a windowpane, not a broken windowpane could be seen. We had-- had to be fixed. We had to pay for it, I mean, everybody whose house was destroyed, and there were quite a few. Had to be done, all over Germany. And within two weeks it had to be done. And our former chauffeur, Hans Aretz, came and did it during the night, fixed what he could. We all worked. And in the morning he went to his job. That was the kind of human being he was, a wonderful-- my best friend, all the time, even when I was a little girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what happened to people who couldn't fix things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they had to. There wasn't even-- people whose houses were destroyed could afford to fix it. They didn't go into the apartments. They were-- they-- that was all in the suburbs. And they could afford it, like ourselves. They-- there was-- I never heard of anybody who was not financially-- otherwise the Jewish community would have helped out. It had to be fixed. And most of the men were gone. They went-- they were-- they were taken to Dachau. And at that time my father was not taken. It was later.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this time your grandmother was living with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- you know what? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My god. I can't remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nobody ever asked me that before. I don't know where she was that night. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My god.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you returned, was she home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody was in the house. Her room-- the-- her room was totally destroyed. The pictures of her parents-- they had stabbed with-- with whatever into the eyes and the mouth and the heart. It was destroyed, both her parents' pictures. I don't know. Honestly, isn't that awful?

speaker: interviewer
text: But later you got together again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes. That's terrible. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Maybe she was-- maybe my parents took care of her before because of my fear. And-- and maybe she went to-- to a cousin or friend's house. We were very self-involved. And I was a-- I'm not trying to apologize, but I was a teenager, and I was very much involved with myself and my parents at that time. And I don't think I ever wrote it in my diary either. And I have that. I know it by heart, because I've read that part of what happened on the 9th and 10th of November so many, many times in front of classes, in front of adults, in front-- so I know it practically by heart. And I'm not-- my grandmother's not mentioned in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did Kristallnacht affect the rest of your stay in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those of you who-- I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it affect your life while you stayed still in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had to move. We had to get-- we had a family that was-- that was waiting for their-- for their departure to the United States. They already had their visa, Oscar and Sarah Kompatz and their son Wolf, who live-- he lives in Los Angeles now. They gave us their apartment, and they moved up into the attic. We had one kitchen, one bathroom. And they were very kind. We had nowhere to go, no-- no-- nowhere to go. And at that time, I decided I had to do something to get out of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when did your father stop working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I would say it was sometime in 1936. They could-- I remember very distinctly that the manufacturer-- the head of the manufacturing plant, came. And later-- and he came, they went into the [GERMAN] and talked. And then he left, and I heard my father crying in the room. And then he told us that he cannot work any-- it's finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Drove everybody crazy. It was very difficult. He was not used to being home. And by the time-- and he was very-- and we understood, but we also had our lives to live. I was cleaning the house like that was the most important thing in the-- in the world, and it was never good enough. I could not do what two maids did all those years. I tried. Then I always thought I was doing good enough job, became very self-conscious of how limited it was. And we had a lot of arguments, my father and I. But yeah-- but then shortly after that, I went to another school. I left to Krefeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your sister behave through this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister? Like I said, I was very self-involved, very much so. And my sister also had to stop school. She couldn't continue school. And we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was helping a little bit. I don't know. I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the final decision made that you are going to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- Germany? Well, first I went to a-- a school in Wolfratshausen, near Munich. Very shortly--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- March of 1939-- '38. And I went to school in Wolfratshausen. I was sent there. I was very homesick. I was there for six months, and then I came home, came back. And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] and that's when I made the decision I have to do something. Shortly after that, I heard that-- like I told you, the-- our quota number was so high for my parents it didn't look very good. Yes, and then my father was arrested in the middle of the night. That was in February--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was after--

speaker: interviewee
text: --of '38, after the Kristallnacht. We were-- we were living already in that apartment. And I woke up in the middle of the night, and I heard voices. And-- and that's what this movie is about, the video that they made of me. I looked out the door, and there was my father dressed up, and there were two men standing there, one in Nazi uniform and the other one civilian. And I realized-- that's when I realized they were taking him after all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you there when they took him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I saw it. I saw it. And then he-- they-- they left. And he-- and Vati said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that he did something that-- he-- he-- he made it clear that we knew nothing about it. My father tried to get some cash money and some silver cutlery, sterling silver cutlery, that he was hoping, if we ever get out, that he was able to sell. And they caught this man who did-- who tried to smuggle it out of Germany at the border. My father was arrested. He was put into prison. And three months later, I heard that if a girl's 18 year old and over, could apply to England to become a maid in a English household. Without telling my parents, I applied for that, and I lied about my age. And not very long after, I got a special delivery letter that I was accepted.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was only for Jewish girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, anybody could. But no-- no-- no Gentile girls would apply, mostly Jewish-- only Jewish girls applied.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop again for a minute.
